title: ⚔️ Chapter Boss ⭐⭐⭐: Ellipsis, Substitution and Cohesion

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses verbal ellipsis CORRECTLY?

options.text: He writes faster than she writes.
He writes faster than she does.
He writes faster than she write.
He writes faster than she.

questions.explanation: "Than she does" correctly uses verbal ellipsis — "does" stands in for the omitted "writes". This is a natural, standard form. (a) repeats "writes" unnecessarily. (c) uses the base form incorrectly. (d) drops the verb entirely without a substitute, creating an ambiguous form that is grammatically incomplete in formal written English.

questions.prompt: Find the sentence with an INCORRECT substitution.

options.text: "Are you going to the meeting?" "I expect so."
She loves hiking and so does her partner.
"Did they win?" "I believe not."
They didn't finish on time, and I didn't too.

questions.explanation: The error is (d): for negative agreement, you need "Neither did I" or "Nor did I" — not "I didn't too". "Too" is used for positive agreement only ("She came, and I did too"). (a), (b), and (c) all use substitution correctly: so after expect (clausal), so does after positive statement, not after believe (negative clausal).

questions.prompt:
Read: "Several anomalies were found in the data. ___ anomalies had been present since the original dataset was compiled."
Which is the BEST choice for the gap?

options.text: These
Such
Some
Both These and Such are correct.

questions.explanation: Both "These anomalies" (demonstrative pronoun pointing back to the specific anomalies just mentioned) and "Such anomalies" (formal substitution meaning anomalies of that kind) are correct and create good cohesion. "Some" would imply only a portion of the anomalies, not the specific ones just referenced.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an appropriate cohesive device to introduce an EXAMPLE?

options.text: Many animals migrate seasonally. Consequently, the Arctic tern travels from pole to pole.
Many animals migrate seasonally. For instance, the Arctic tern travels from pole to pole.
Many animals migrate seasonally. Nevertheless, the Arctic tern travels from pole to pole.
Many animals migrate seasonally. However, the Arctic tern travels from pole to pole.

questions.explanation: "For instance" introduces a specific example — perfect for illustrating the general claim about seasonal migration. "Consequently" signals a result (wrong); "Nevertheless" signals contrast (wrong — the tern's migration is not a contrasting exception); "However" also signals contrast (also wrong).

questions.prompt:
Read: "The CEO announced a restructuring plan. The plan involved redundancies. The redundancies were unexpected. The unexpected redundancies shocked employees."
Which rewrite BEST uses cohesive devices and reference to avoid repetition?

options.text: The CEO announced a restructuring plan. The plan involved redundancies, and these were unexpected, shocking employees.
The CEO announced a restructuring plan. This involved redundancies, which, being unexpected, shocked employees.
The CEO announced a restructuring plan involving redundancies. These proved unexpected and shocked employees.
All three options (a), (b), and (c) are valid rewrites.

questions.explanation: All three rewrites correctly use cohesive devices: (a) uses "these" (demonstrative reference); (b) uses "This" (demonstrative) and "which" (relative clause); (c) uses "These" (demonstrative) and a participial clause. All three eliminate unnecessary repetition while maintaining clear meaning. At C2, multiple valid cohesion strategies exist — recognising them all is the mastery point.

questions.prompt: Identify the TYPE of ellipsis in: "She can play the violin and [play] the cello."

options.text: Nominal ellipsis — a noun has been omitted.
Clausal ellipsis — an entire clause has been omitted.
Verbal ellipsis — a verb phrase has been omitted.
This is not ellipsis — it is a substitution.

questions.explanation: The omitted element is the verb "play" — a verb/predicate element. This is verbal ellipsis: the auxiliary "can" and the verb "play" are omitted from the second conjunct because they have already appeared in the first. The noun (the cello) remains. No substitution pro-form is used — the element is simply left out.
